narrative: As a Hispanic male applying to law school, you've shared your strong undergraduate GPA of 3.30—which is actually above the typical 3.21 average—and a solid LSAT score of 36, close to the usual 36.70 benchmark, along with your plan to attend full-time like most applicants. However, the admissions model, which predicts the likelihood of passing the bar exam on the first try based on profiles like yours, estimated a 58.7% chance that you would fail it. This tipped the prediction toward failure, leading to the rejection of your application, as the school prioritizes candidates shown to have a strong shot at bar success. The most influential factor pushing toward this failure prediction was your Year 3 school decile ranking of 2nd, which is notably lower than the average 5.53 and signaled weaker performance in later undergraduate years compared to peers. Nearly as important was your Year 1 decile of 1st, also well below the typical 5.71 average, suggesting early academic struggles that carried weight in the model's eyes. On a more positive note, your LSAT score worked strongly in your favor, pulling the prediction toward expecting you to pass by demonstrating the analytical skills that often correlate with bar success. Your lower-middle family income bracket played a lesser role here, but overall, these early and mid-undergrad rankings stood out as the top drivers, placing you below typical students who rank higher in those deciles and thus show steadier academic trajectories. While your GPA and LSAT are competitive, the model saw your decile positions as red flags compared to the stronger upward trends of admitted applicants. This doesn't define your potential—many overcome similar starts—but it explains the cautious decision. To move forward, consider retaking courses or pursuing a post-baccalaureate program to boost your later-year rankings, perhaps targeting LSAT retakes for an even higher edge, and reapply with updated transcripts that show improvement. Building a narrative of academic resilience, especially as a first-generation or Hispanic applicant, could also strengthen future applications through essays or recommendations. With targeted steps, you can shift these predictions in your favor next time.
